summary: Restored matching status in configure.py for several item object files that had been marked NonMatching by PR#2203. The change is metadata/build-list only: it flips specific melee/it/items Object entries back to Matching and removes the separate itchicoritaleaf.c Object line. decomp.dev reported GALE01 linked code/data increases after merge, but there was no PR body or human review discussion explaining the regression.
what_changed: In configure.py's MatchingFor object list, restored Object(Matching, ...) for melee/it/items/itheart.c.
In the Pokemon item section, restored Object(Matching, ...) for melee/it/items/itchicorita.c.
Removed the separate Object(NonMatching, "melee/it/items/itchicoritaleaf.c") line from the configure.py object list.
Restored Object(Matching, ...) for melee/it/items/itthunder.c and melee/it/items/itfushigibana.c.
smart_moves: Kept the fix limited to configure.py object classification, which directly addressed removed match accounting without touching C source.
Restored known-matching item objects by flipping Object(NonMatching, path) back to Object(Matching, path), making the intent easy to audit.
Grouped the restoration in the existing item and Pokemon sections of MatchingFor instead of reorganizing the object list.
Let the decomp.dev GALE01 report validate the effect: linked code rose by 15772 bytes and linked data by 1008 bytes.
decomp_lessons: Matching progress can regress from build metadata changes alone; check configure.py Object(Matching)/Object(NonMatching) status when reported linked-code percentages unexpectedly drop.
Small configure-only PRs are useful for restoring accounting when source already matches and a prior PR accidentally demoted object files.

When modifying item object lists, audit neighboring entries for linked or combined build-unit assumptions; this PR removed itchicoritaleaf.c while restoring itchicorita.c, but the dump does not include a human explanation for that choice.
Future restorations should cite the prior regression PR and rely on decomp.dev linked-code/data deltas to confirm that matching status was restored.
naming_conventions: Item source files live under melee/it/items and use the it* prefix, such as itheart.c, ittomato.c, itbox.c, itharisen.c, itlipstick.c, and itmariocape.c.

Pokemon item/object filenames in this area include Japanese/species-derived names such as ittosakinto.c, itchicorita.c, itkabigon.c, itkamex.c, itmatadogas.c, itlizardon.c, itthunder.c, itfreezer.c, ithassam.c, itporygon2.c, itmaril.c, and itfushigibana.c.
configure.py tracks per-object match status with Object(Matching, "path") and Object(NonMatching, "path") inside MatchingFor(*versions).
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No assembly or C matching tactics were shown in the PR; the matching tactic was restoring build/configure classifications.
Use configure.py status flips as the appropriate fix when an object is already matching but was accidentally counted as NonMatching.
Use decomp.dev's GALE01 linked code/data report as a sanity check for matching-accounting changes.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the PR slice.
decomp-dev[bot] reported for GALE01: linked code 31.03% (+0.41%, +15772 bytes) and linked data 28.88% (+0.08%, +1008 bytes).
follow_up_queries: Why did PR#2203 demote these item objects from Matching to NonMatching?
Verify whether itchicoritaleaf.c is intentionally folded into or excluded alongside itchicorita.c in the current configure.py object list.
Audit recent configure.py changes for accidental Object(Matching) to Object(NonMatching) regressions in melee/it/items.
Compare decomp.dev GALE01 reports before and after PR#2213 to confirm restored matching accounting.
